So, well, that team actually, they cover digital marketing, but they'll do things like pricing and packaging.
And then more traditional, like private equity value add stuff like M&A, capital markets.
My favorite story of them, though, is, I don't know, do you know Chess.com?
No, I think so.
Chess.com is the biggest online chess app in the world.
They're one of our portfolio companies.
I figured it was something along those lines, but I didn't want to risk it.
Yeah, right.
It does tell a little bit of the story, but they are a freemium model.
A lot of GA's investments are a freemium model.
And they were trying to figure out what features do I put in which part of my pricing.
And when you are a former grandmaster chess player and you're very focused on cultivating the best chess experience and product out there, pricing is not something you want to spend your time on.
So they kicked it to Setchkin's team, who went through and did a bunch of complex analysis.
They actually hired the former head of pricing from McKinsey to lead that team.
And they did their math, came up with an answer, and then moved some features around.
And you had a bunch of people who said when they did their survey work, they said that game review, basically, how did I play in my chess match, was the most important feature.
And yet it was at the bottom in terms of what people were paying for it.
So you flip that, and all of a sudden you have a bunch of people upgrading and nobody is churning.
That's kind of the example that we like to tell on the pricing side.
But Tetchkin leads the digital marketing efforts, and he was the one who was singing your guys' praises.
That's really cool.
That's awesome because that's one of the things that I was so excited.

okay.

a little bit of the GA spiel, and then I want to hear all about it.

can get into that too, but we're excited to, you know, you're building in public so we can see the numbers.

I've got of two sides to the business, right?
One side is the lane that

Outbound channels, sales lad motions are expensive.

If they took 10% of their annual budget, they could essentially build a new New York Times about a couple times a year.

And so, you know, it's kind of always been puzzling to me why you have companies like Hems and Hers and others that are like great companies where like they're spending 400 million a year in marketing and ads.
And you're like, why would you not just like own that audience and build that audience and own it, you know?
And it's just like being in charge of your own destiny and the companies that have done that, like Canva.com or Wise.com, they've grown tremendously.
Highly profitable, you know, CAC, you know, and it's like, it just shows that.
So I've been doing that for every company I've worked at.
Lucky enough to be in several hyper-growth companies like HashiCorp, ServiceSight, and Scaled.
And then DeepGram has started taking everything I learned and building these workflows and then putting editors and humans in the loop to kind of power that process, right?
So everything I would normally do where, you know, it would be very manual and having to hire a whole team, I kind of supercharged by all taking my expertise into workflows.
And then from there, I started building these workshops, and we had over 170 people that paid for them.
And it was kind of like the biggest product market fit, like, engine ever.
You know, you're spending a whole day teaching people how to do something, and then by the end of it, they're like, can just do this for me?
This is amazing.
And, you know, and that's how we got our first two customers, including Kyle, who now heads up all our client accounts.

Becoming our first client and working with us.
We didn't poach him, he joined us.

And I think we started to see a lot more validation of like, and a lot of our belief is not only AI or experts, it's like how those two can work together when it comes to knowledge work, right?

Like they want to get the results.
want to get, you know, to work right away.

None of them.

from their CEO.

Our average ACV right now, our ACV is about 160,000.

Right.
So that's what

But then, like, the part that's, like, really, really interesting that I'll show you is, like, what we built under the hood, though, is essentially as we were building these workflows, we realized that the process of building workflows is the part that was the hardest in adapting these workflows from companies.
So what we actually built is an AI coding agent that codes workflows and represents these workflows in code and then a runtime layer that actually runs those workflows, right?
So just to kind of give you an example here, like, these are all our workflows running right now, so let's just say.
Okay, like, let me find one, like, that's running right now, like, this one is, like, taking feedback from one of our editors, and then reprocessing the whole, the whole article, right?
Like, we have, like, one that's vetting everyone that's applying for jobs, you know, it goes through, and this is, like, an internal one that we use for ourselves, right?
Presses the resume, gets the job requirements, analyzes it, looks at the answers to all the questions they posed, and then makes a final assessment, whether to move them along or not.
And then you have a recruiter in the loop that reviews this, right?
And so, like, essentially, all of these are, like, workflows that were created with an expert plus our coding agent.
And so then, like, we're running this at a massive scale, right?
So if you look at, for example, like, our topic workflow, these are, like, all the steps of breaking down the research.
And then we have a runtime layer that's.
Running all of this.
And this is where it kind of is very different.
The whole idea is if you think about the productivity of your average developer in the last 10 years and how much that has increased.
So just think about your average developer or software engineer using Cursor, obviously GitHub, and a bunch of other tooling versus that person 10 years ago.
They're at least 10 to 100x more productive in what they can do and the speed that they can do those things.
And then if you look at an average knowledge worker, I just think of myself, for example.
I don't think I'm anywhere near that much more productive than I was 10 years ago.
Maybe I communicate a little bit better.
Maybe I have a little bit easier time finding information.
But I haven't learned necessarily from every single pull request of my work.
But if all my work was represented in code,
And all my activities were in a file, like I could feed that to an LLM, I can feed that to an agentic workflow, and actually it could be really, really powerful.
And that's essentially what we built.
We built like a, call it an AI workflow code engine, so that you almost have a config file for all the different jobs to be done internally.

is, and the one.
